planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring after frost danger passes in moist, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in full sun to partial afternoon shade; tibouchina requires consistently moist soil and an acidic reaction to thrive. Chlorosis (yellow leaves with green veins) indicates iron deficiency from overly alkaline soil.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to partial afternoon shade; princess flower is one of the most eye-catching flowering shrubs available for zone 9–11 gardens, producing large (3–4 inch), intensely saturated violet-purple flowers with prominent red stamens almost continuously from spring through frost. The soft, velvety leaves with prominent veining provide beautiful texture between bloom cycles, and new leaves often have a reddish tinge. Prune back by one-third in early spring to keep the plant compact and encourage maximum branching and flower production — unpruned plants become leggy. Maintain acidic soil pH (5.5–6.5) for best performance; apply chelated iron if leaves yellow with green veins. In zone 9a it may suffer frost damage in hard winters but often regrows from the base. Tibouchina is outstanding as a specimen plant, in large containers, or as an informal hedge where its brilliant purple flowers create a stunning focal point.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

common_name: Ixora (Flame of the Woods, Jungle Geranium)

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 5.0–6.0), humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; ixora is highly sensitive to alkaline soil and high pH — in alkaline or limestone soils, apply acidifying fertilizer and chelated micronutrients regularly to prevent the severe chlorosis that is the most common problem with this plant in the Southeast.

directions: Plant in strongly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; ixora is one of the most popular tropical flowering shrubs in the world, producing dense, rounded clusters of small tubular flowers in brilliant shades of red, orange, yellow, pink, and white nearly year-round in frost-free gardens and spring through fall in zone 9. The flat-topped flower clusters (corymbs) are 3–4 inches across and attract butterflies throughout the bloom season. It is a slow-growing, compact shrub and one of the best choices for formal hedges and foundation plantings in tropical and subtropical landscapes. The main cultural challenge is soil pH: ixora demands strongly acidic conditions (pH 5.0–6.0) and in the alkaline limestone soils of Florida and the Gulf Coast, iron and manganese deficiencies cause chronic yellowing. Use acidifying fertilizer specially formulated for ixora and apply chelated iron if chlorosis appears. Avoid 'lollipop pruning' (shearing into balls) which reduces blooming — prune lightly by selectively removing branches.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; brunfelsia prefers bright, indirect light or morning sun with afternoon shade in the heat of zone 9. All parts of the plant are toxic to pets, particularly dogs — the alkaloid brunfelsamidine causes severe neurological symptoms.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; yesterday-today-and-tomorrow takes its name from one of the most enchanting flower color-change sequences in horticulture: the fragrant, flat-faced flowers open deep violet-purple ('yesterday'), fade to lavender ('today'), and turn white ('tomorrow') before dropping — so at any given moment a single plant displays all three colors simultaneously, creating a remarkable polychrome effect. The sweetly fragrant flowers appear in spring and intermittently through summer and fall. This attractive, moderate-growing evergreen shrub performs best in the bright shade of a pergola or lath house where it is protected from scorching afternoon sun. A critical caution: all parts of the plant are highly toxic to dogs, causing severe neurological symptoms including tremors and seizures — it should not be planted where dogs can access the fruit or flowers. Prune lightly after the main spring bloom flush.
sources: American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring after temperatures are consistently above 60°F in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; crotons require consistent warmth and thrive in hot, humid, tropical weather. Cold below 50°F causes leaf drop. The milky sap is irritating — wear gloves when pruning.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade — the most vibrant leaf coloration develops in brighter light; deep shade produces greener, less intensely colored foliage. Garden croton is one of the most spectacularly colorful foliage plants in tropical horticulture, with leathery leaves that combine vivid red, orange, yellow, green, and purple in patterns ranging from fine-speckled splashes to broad blotches, depending on the hundreds of named cultivars available. Hundreds of leaf shapes exist: broad and lobed, narrow and twisted, curled, or oak-leaf shaped. It thrives in the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast and Florida in full outdoor culture in zone 9–12. Mite infestations are the most common pest problem, particularly in dry conditions — maintain adequate soil moisture and mist foliage during droughts. Crotons drop leaves readily when moved, so establish carefully. An outstanding specimen and landscape plant for bold tropical color.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

common_name: Pride of Barbados (Red Bird of Paradise)

planting.note: Direct sow scarified or nicked seed 1 inch deep after the last frost, or start indoors 6–8 weeks earlier; pride of barbados germinates rapidly after scarification in warm soil. The plant dies back to the ground after hard freezes in zone 8 but regrows vigorously from established roots and can bloom within the first season from seed.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil — sandy, rocky, and even alkaline soils are acceptable; pride of barbados is one of the most spectacular flowering tropical shrubs for hot, dry, difficult sites, producing large showy clusters of exotic-looking flowers in brilliant shades of red-orange with long protruding red stamens (resembling a bird of paradise arrangement) over finely pinnate, lacy green foliage from summer through frost. It is exceptionally heat, drought, and poor-soil tolerant and blooms most prolifically in the hottest part of the summer when many other plants flag. The entire shrub is typically killed to the ground by hard freezes in zone 8 but regrows robustly each spring, often re-blooming within 60 days. In zone 9–12 it is woody and persistent, reaching shrub-tree size. Seeds germinate quickly after the hard seed coat is nicked or soaked. All parts are toxic if ingested. An outstanding butterfly and hummingbird plant for xeric, full-sun gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow seed or plant container-grown plants in spring after frost danger passes in any well-drained soil in full sun; esperanza grows rapidly from seed and can bloom the first year. It dies back to the ground after hard freezes in zone 8 but regrows strongly from established roots in spring — do not cut back until new growth confirms the roots have survived.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained to dry soil — sandy, rocky, alkaline, and poor soils are all acceptable; esperanza or yellow bells is a native flowering shrub and small tree of Texas, the Southwest, and Mexico that produces brilliant golden-yellow trumpet-shaped flowers in large clusters almost continuously from late spring through frost. It is among the most heat tolerant and drought resistant flowering plants available for zone 8–11 landscapes, thriving in conditions that challenge almost everything else. The showy flowers attract hummingbirds and butterflies prolifically throughout the summer. The cultivar 'Gold Star' is the most widely planted, selected for compact habit and extra-large flower clusters. Esperanza grows vigorously and may need annual cutting back in mild winters; in zone 9 it is woody and persistent year-round while in zone 8 it typically dies to the ground in winter and regrows from roots. It blooms on new wood, so frost damage sets back the plant temporarily, not permanently.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; shrimp plant grows rapidly and roots easily from stem cuttings — pinch tips frequently during the first few months to encourage branching and a fuller habit before allowing it to bloom.

directions: Plant in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; shrimp plant is one of the most charming and long-blooming perennials for shaded Gulf Coast gardens, named for the distinctive overlapping reddish-brown bracts (modified leaves) that form arching, drooping spikes 3–4 inches long, closely resembling a cooked shrimp — from the tip of each bract emerges a delicate white two-lipped flower. Bloom is essentially continuous from spring through frost and often persists in warm winters. It performs equally well in light shade or partial sun, making it versatile for difficult spots under the canopy of large trees. Hummingbirds visit the flowers readily. A yellow-bracted form ('Fruit Cocktail' or 'Yellow Queen') provides the same long bloom season in a lighter color. Pinch the plant back after bloom flushes to keep it compact and stimulate fresh growth. Propagate from stem cuttings, which root readily in moist soil.

planting.note: Plant rooted hardwood cuttings in spring in moist, fertile, well-drained, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; angel's trumpets are heavy feeders and heavy drinkers — they require frequent watering and regular fertilization to produce the large, lush growth and abundant blooms they are capable of. All parts of the plant are highly toxic — do not grow where children or pets may ingest any part.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; angel's trumpet is among the most spectacular flowering plants in temperate-tropical horticulture, producing enormous (10–20 inch) pendulous trumpet-shaped flowers in white, yellow, peach, pink, or salmon that hang gracefully downward and fill the air with an intoxicating fragrance, particularly in the evening and night. With adequate water and nutrition, a well-established plant can produce 50–100 blooms simultaneously at peak season. Brugmansia is distinguished from Datura by its pendulous (not erect) flowers and woody, tree-like habit. All parts of the plant are extremely toxic — containing tropane alkaloids including scopolamine, atropine, and hyoscyamine — and should never be grown where children or pets may contact or ingest any part. In zone 8 it may die back in hard winters but regrows from established roots; in zone 9–11 it is evergreen and can be trained as a small tree. Propagate from 12-inch hardwood cuttings placed directly in moist soil.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; night-blooming jasmine grows rapidly and roots readily from cuttings. The flowers open only at night and their fragrance can be overwhelming in enclosed spaces — site where air circulation carries the scent rather than concentrating it.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; night-blooming jasmine (not a true jasmine, despite the common name) is one of the most intensely fragrant plants in the world — the small, tubular, greenish-white flowers that open at night release a perfume so powerful that a single shrub can scent an entire garden and be detected from 200 feet away. The fragrance is often described as intoxicating and slightly narcotic. Blooms appear in recurring flushes from spring through fall, with clusters of small whitish berries following each bloom cycle that attract birds. Despite its name it is easy to grow, thriving in nearly any well-drained soil in heat and humidity. Prune back by one-third in early spring to maintain a tidy, compact shape and encourage vigorous blooming growth. Site carefully — the fragrance is spectacular outdoors but can be overwhelming in enclosed spaces. In zone 8 it may suffer frost damage but typically regrows from the roots.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in any well-drained soil in full sun; cape honeysuckle is extremely adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions and is virtually foolproof in warm climates. Train the scrambling stems onto a support structure early — the plant does not twine or cling on its own and must be tied.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil — poor, sandy, alkaline, and rocky soils are all acceptable; cape honeysuckle is not a true honeysuckle but a South African scrambling shrub that produces clusters of brilliant orange-red tubular flowers almost continuously from late fall through spring in zone 9 — filling the garden with color exactly when other flowering plants are dormant. It is extraordinary as a hummingbird and butterfly plant. The plant does not twine or cling like a vine but scrambles vigorously through other shrubs or must be tied to a trellis or fence; alternatively it makes an excellent sprawling groundcover on slopes or a large informal mound-shaped specimen shrub. A yellow-orange cultivar ('Aurea') is also available. Cape honeysuckle withstands heat, drought, salt spray, and poor soil with ease once established and grows rapidly in warm weather. In zone 8 it may suffer significant frost damage but recovers vigorously from roots in spring.
